---
title: "Venues"
description: "A research-facing venue map showing where theorem-heavy, optimization-heavy, graph-heavy, and statistics-heavy work is commonly published, and how venue fit depends on audience and evidence."
page-type: research-direction
status: published
update-status: monitor
level: research
topic: venues
prerequisites:
  - /research/index.qmd
  - /research/theorem-families.qmd
  - /research/directions.qmd
  - /research/surveys.qmd
learning-goals:
  - "Understand venue fit as a question of audience, contribution type, and evidence, not prestige alone."
  - "Recognize the main venue clusters around ML theory, optimization, graph learning, and probability/statistics."
  - "See how the site's research directions map onto different conference and journal cultures."
  - "Choose better reading targets by looking at venue family rather than isolated paper titles."
applications:
  - literature-entry
  - publication-awareness
  - machine-learning-theory
  - optimization
  - statistics
keywords:
  - venues
  - conference map
  - journals
  - literature culture
maintainer: mtuann
last-reviewed: 2026-04-25
source-check-date: 2026-04-25
review-cadence: quarterly
date-modified: last-modified
draft: false
stable-sources:
  - "Research index"
  - "Directions"
  - "Surveys"
current-sources:
  - "NeurIPS official site"
  - "ICML official site"
  - "ICLR official site"
  - "Association for Computational Learning"
  - "JMLR"
  - "PMLR"
---

## Why This Page

This page is not a ranking guide.

It is a `venue fit` guide.

The point is not to ask "which venue is best?" in the abstract. The useful question is:

`For this kind of result, who is the audience and what kind of evidence do they expect?`

That question helps with both paper reading and research orientation.

It tells you:

- where a direction tends to live
- how theorem-heavy and experiment-heavy work are blended
- which papers are likely to be good reading targets for your current background

## Venue Snapshot

- `Type`: top-level research map
- `Setting`: readers trying to understand where different kinds of work are typically published
- `Main claim`: venue names are most useful when read as signals about audience and evidence culture
- `Why it matters`: different venue families reward different balances of theorem, experiment, systems, and application content

## How To Use This Page

When you see a paper, ask four questions:

1. `What is the main contribution type?`
   theorem, algorithm, benchmark, system, application, or synthesis

2. `What evidence carries the paper?`
   proofs, experiments, ablations, empirical evaluation, or some mix

3. `Who is the natural audience?`
   learning theorists, broad ML readers, optimization people, statisticians, graph-learning researchers

4. `What is the surrounding literature culture?`
   conference-first, journal-first, or mixed

Venue fit becomes much clearer after those questions.

## Cluster 1: Broad ML Conference Venues

### Typical venues

- `NeurIPS`
- `ICML`
- `ICLR`
- `AISTATS`

### Best for

- broad machine learning interest
- papers mixing theory and experiment
- model, method, or representation contributions with strong ML relevance
- active frontier work where fast iteration matters

### Evidence culture

These venues often reward a good blend of:

- conceptual novelty
- strong experimental story
- enough theory to clarify or support the main mechanism when theory is relevant

This does not mean all papers look the same, but it does mean the audience is broader than a theorem-only community.

### Where it appears in this site

- [Directions](directions.qmd)
- [Applications > Machine Learning](../applications/machine-learning/index.qmd)
- [How Top-Venue Papers Are Shaped](../publication/how-top-venue-papers-are-shaped.qmd)

## Cluster 2: Learning-Theory Venues

### Typical venues

- `COLT`
- `JMLR`
- theory-heavy tracks or theory-oriented papers in broad ML venues

### Best for

- theorem-first contributions
- generalization, sample complexity, optimization theory, and online learning
- work where mathematical clarity is the main contribution

### Evidence culture

Here theorems usually carry more weight than large experimental campaigns.

But readers still need to ask:

- what assumptions make the result meaningful?
- is the theorem illuminating a practical regime or only a stylized one?
- if experiments exist, are they illustrating the mathematics or replacing missing theory?

### Where it appears in this site

- [Theorem Families](theorem-families.qmd)
- [Directions](directions.qmd)
- [AI / ML Theory roadmap](../roadmaps/ai-ml-theory.qmd)

## Cluster 3: Optimization And Numerical Venues

### Typical venues

- `Mathematical Programming`
- `SIAM Journal on Optimization`
- `Mathematical Programming Computation`
- optimization-related work in `NeurIPS`, `ICML`, or engineering conferences when ML relevance is central

### Best for

- optimization theory
- solver design
- duality and certificate structure
- numerical methods with strong mathematical analysis
- differentiable optimization in research areas that still care about solver behavior

### Evidence culture

These venues often care more about:

- formulation clarity
- assumptions and proof quality
- computational behavior tied to the theory

than about large benchmark collections alone.

### Where it appears in this site

- [Optimization](../topics/advanced/optimization/index.qmd)
- [Duality and Certificates](../topics/advanced/optimization/concepts/duality-and-certificates.qmd)
- [Optimization for Machine Learning](../applications/machine-learning/optimization-for-machine-learning.qmd)

## Cluster 4: Statistics And Probability Venues

### Typical venues

- `Annals of Statistics`
- `Annals of Probability`
- `JASA`
- `JRSS B`
- `Bernoulli`
- `AISTATS` when the work has strong ML overlap

### Best for

- estimation theory
- uncertainty and calibration
- high-dimensional inference
- asymptotic and non-asymptotic statistical analysis
- probability tools that support modern data science

### Evidence culture

These venues often read very differently from broad ML conferences.

The audience usually expects:

- sharper attention to assumptions
- stronger inferential interpretation
- more careful statistical framing

### Where it appears in this site

- [Statistics](../topics/foundation/statistics/index.qmd)
- [Probability](../topics/foundation/probability/index.qmd)
- [Uncertainty Calibration and Predictive Confidence](../applications/machine-learning/uncertainty-calibration-and-predictive-confidence.qmd)

## Cluster 5: Algorithms And Theory-CS Venues

### Typical venues

- `STOC`
- `FOCS`
- `SODA`
- related theory workshops and journals

### Best for

- algorithmic guarantees
- complexity results
- lower bounds and impossibility results
- sketching, streaming, and discrete-math flavored theory

### Evidence culture

Compared with broad ML venues, these communities often place more emphasis on:

- theorem novelty
- proof architecture
- asymptotic guarantees
- formal model choice

### Where it appears in this site

- [Theorem Families](theorem-families.qmd)
- [Paper Lab](../paper-lab/index.qmd)
- future `Discrete Math` and algorithms-adjacent parts of the site

## Cluster 6: Graph And Data-Mining Venues

### Typical venues

- `KDD`
- `WWW`
- graph-learning work also appears in `NeurIPS`, `ICML`, and `ICLR`

### Best for

- graph mining and representation learning
- web-scale relational problems
- recommendation and network analysis
- graph methods where application framing matters strongly

### Evidence culture

These venues often care about:

- the graph problem setting itself
- scale and datasets
- application relevance
- whether the method makes sense for real networked data

### Where it appears in this site

- [Directions](directions.qmd)
- [CS224W 2024](https://snap.stanford.edu/class/cs224w-2024/)
- [Graph Rewiring, Homophily, and Heterophily](../applications/machine-learning/graph-rewiring-homophily-and-heterophily.qmd)

## How The Site's Directions Map To Venue Clusters

### High-dimensional probability and random matrices

Often lives across:

- probability and statistics journals
- `COLT`
- `JMLR`
- some theory-heavy papers in broad ML venues

### Modern learning theory

Often lives across:

- `COLT`
- `JMLR`
- theory-facing papers in `NeurIPS`, `ICML`, and sometimes `ICLR`

### Graph learning beyond simple message passing

Often lives across:

- `NeurIPS`
- `ICML`
- `ICLR`
- `KDD`
- `WWW`

### Generative modeling through score, flow, and transport

Often lives across:

- `NeurIPS`
- `ICML`
- `ICLR`
- `JMLR`

### Optimization inside learning and inference pipelines

Often lives across:

- optimization journals
- `NeurIPS`
- `ICML`
- engineering and control venues when the problem framing demands it

### Representation geometry and in-context structure

Often lives across:

- `ICLR`
- `NeurIPS`
- `ICML`
- `JMLR`

## Common Venue Mistakes

### Mistake 1: reading venue name as a quality oracle

Venue names are signals about audience and expectations, not replacements for technical judgment.

### Mistake 2: assuming theorem-heavy and benchmark-heavy papers are judged the same way

Different venue families tolerate different balances of proof, experiment, and systems detail.

### Mistake 3: ignoring journals

If you only read conference papers, you miss a large amount of strong work in optimization, probability, and statistics.

### Mistake 4: using venue as the only way to choose papers

Venue fit helps, but reading trails, theorem families, and surveys are still better first tools than prestige-chasing.

## What To Learn Next

- [Surveys](surveys.qmd), if you want literature entry points before venue-specific reading
- [How Top-Venue Papers Are Shaped](../publication/how-top-venue-papers-are-shaped.qmd), if you want to understand the paper-construction side
- [Claim-Evidence Matrix](../publication/claim-evidence-matrix.qmd), if you want to analyze how venue expectations affect paper structure

## Sources And Further Reading

- [NeurIPS](https://neurips.cc/) - `Paper bridge` - official home for one of the main broad ML conference venues. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [ICML](https://icml.cc/) - `Paper bridge` - official home for a major broad ML venue with a wide algorithm-and-theory mix. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [ICLR](https://iclr.cc/) - `Paper bridge` - official home for the major representation-learning conference with strong deep-learning presence. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Association for Computational Learning / COLT](https://www.learningtheory.org/) - `Paper bridge` - official home for the learning-theory conference family. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Journal of Machine Learning Research](https://jmlr.org/) - `Second pass` - important journal venue for theory, methodology, and broader ML work. Checked `2026-04-25`.
- [Proceedings of Machine Learning Research](https://proceedings.mlr.press/) - `Second pass` - official proceedings hub for many ML and statistics conferences, including AISTATS and COLT. Checked `2026-04-25`.
